nodes.annotation: Starting position. The Jobava London begins with 1.d4, aiming for a unique setup with Nc3 and Bf4.
1.d4 — Claiming the center. The Jobava diverges from the standard London by developing the knight to c3 instead of d2.
1...d5 — Black's most natural response, contesting the center immediately.
2.Nc3 — The defining move of the Jobava. Unlike the regular London (2.Nf3), the knight goes to c3 to support e4 and create tactical pressure.
2...Nf6 — Black develops naturally. Now White completes the Jobava triangle.
3.Bf4 — The Jobava London setup is complete: d4 + Nc3 + Bf4. White has a flexible position with multiple plans including e3, e4, or Nb5.
3...Bf5 — Black mirrors the bishop development. This looks natural but allows a sharp response.
4.Nb5! — The key move. The knight jumps to b5 threatening Nxc7+ forking king and rook. Black must react immediately.
4...Na6 — The best defense, covering c7. The knight looks awkward on a6 but it's the only way to prevent the fork.
5.e3 — Solid development, preparing Bd3 or Nf3. White maintains the Nb5 pressure.
5...e6 — Black solidifies the center and prepares to develop the dark-squared bishop.
6.Nf3 — White completes development with a comfortable position. The Nb5 still eyes c7 and d6, and White can expand with c4 or a3 next.
4...c6 — A solid alternative to 4...Na6. Black kicks the knight back but spends a tempo.
5.Nc3 — The knight retreats but White has gained time: Black's c6 is slightly passive and blocks the Nb8.
5...e6 — Preparing Bd6 to challenge the Bf4.
6.e3 — Solid structure. White prepares Bd3 to trade off Black's strong bishop.
6...Bd6 — Black challenges the Bf4. This trade is strategically important.
7.Bxd6 — Trading bishops. After this, White's pawn structure is slightly better and the dark squares around Black's king can become weak.
8.Nf3 — White has a pleasant, easy-to-play position. Plans include Bd3 (trading the light bishops), O-O, and later c4 or Ne5.
3...e6 — Black plays solidly, keeping the center closed. This invites the e4 break.
4.e4! — The aggressive central break. This is one of the main ideas behind Nc3 — supporting e4 directly.
4...dxe4 — Black accepts. Declining with 4...Bb4 is the main alternative.
5.Nxe4 — Recapturing with the knight, centralizing powerfully.
5...Nxe4 — Trading knights. After this, White gets the bishop pair.
6.Bd3 — Developing with tempo, eyeing the kingside. White has the bishop pair and a strong center. Plans include Nf3, O-O, and Qe2 with pressure.
3...c5 — Black immediately challenges the d4 pawn. This is aggressive but opens the position for White's better-developed pieces.
4.e3 — Solid. White reinforces d4 and prepares to develop the light-squared bishop.
5.exd4 — Recapturing with the pawn maintains the strong center. White has an IQP (isolated queen pawn) but excellent piece activity.
5...Nc6 — Developing and putting pressure on d4.
6.Nf3 — Natural development. The knight defends d4 and prepares castling.
6...Bg4 — Black pins the knight. This is the critical moment.
7.Be2 — Breaking the pin cleanly. White is ready to castle with a comfortable IQP position. The Bf4 controls key dark squares and the Nc3 supports both d5 and e4 breaks.
Starting position. This line punishes a common club-level mistake.
3...Bg4? — A natural-looking move but a serious mistake! Black develops the bishop to an active square but ignores the Nb5 threat.
4.Nb5! — Threatening Nxc7+ forking king and rook. Black has no good defense because the bishop is on g4 instead of covering c7.
4...Na6 — The only try to cover c7, but it's too late.
5.Nxc7+! — The fork lands! White wins the exchange (rook for knight) with a completely winning position. This is why 3...Bg4 is a blunder in the Jobava.
Starting position. This trap punishes Black's premature central break.
3...e5? — Looks aggressive but it's premature. Black tries to challenge the center immediately but the tactics don't work.
4.dxe5 — Simply taking. The e5 pawn was unprotected.
4...Ng4? — Black tries to recover the pawn by attacking e5, but this walks into a trap.
5.Qd3! — Defends e5 and develops the queen to an active square. Black cannot recover the pawn and is left a pawn down with a worse position. The Ng4 is misplaced and will be kicked by h3.
3...g6 — Black fianchettoes, aiming for a King's Indian-style setup. This is a solid but slightly passive approach.
4.e3 — Keeping things solid. White prepares Bd3 and Nf3 with a comfortable setup.
4...Bg7 — Completing the fianchetto. The bishop looks strong on the long diagonal but d4 is well-protected.
5...O-O — Black castles. The position is solid but White has more space.
6.Be2 — Preparing to castle kingside. White avoids Bd3 here because the bishop would block the d-file.
7.O-O — White castles with a pleasant position. Plans include h3 (preventing ...Bg4), Ne5, and a potential kingside expansion with g4 in some lines.
3...e6 — The most solid response. Black keeps the position closed and prepares Bd6.
4.Nb5 — The thematic jump! Even against ...e6, Nb5 creates problems by threatening Nxc7+ or Nd6+.
4...Na6 — Covering c7. Black's knight is awkwardly placed but it's necessary.
5.e3 — Solid development. White prepares Bd3 and Nf3.
5...Bd6 — Challenging the Bf4. This trade is often in Black's interest.
6.Bxd6 — Trading. White gets a slightly better pawn structure.
6...cxd6 — Recapturing toward the center. Black's d-pawns are doubled but control key central squares.
7.Nf3 — Completing development. White has a comfortable edge with better piece coordination and the Nb5 still exerts pressure.
3...Nbd7 — A flexible response. Black develops the knight to d7 keeping options open for ...e6 or ...c5.
4.e3 — Solid. White prepares to develop the light-squared bishop.
4...e6 — Black solidifies. The position resembles a Colle-Zukertort with reversed colors.
5...Be7 — Modest but solid. Black prepares to castle.
6.Bd3 — Developing the bishop to its best square, eyeing h7.
7.O-O — White castles with a harmonious position. Plans include Ne5, Qf3, and a potential kingside attack. The Bd3 + Bf4 battery is a classic attacking setup.
Starting position. The Caro-Kann is one of the most reliable defenses against 1.e4.
1...c6 — The Caro-Kann! Black prepares ...d5 with pawn support, aiming for a solid center.
2.d4 — White builds the ideal pawn center.
2...d5 — Challenging the center. This is the whole point of 1...c6.
3.Nc3 — The Classical variation. White defends e4 with the knight.
3...dxe4 — Black takes, entering the main line. The alternative 3...Nf6 leads to different structures.
4...Bf5 — The Classical move. Black develops the light-squared bishop BEFORE playing ...e6, which is the key advantage of the Caro-Kann over the French Defense.
5.Ng3 — Attacking the bishop. White wants to drive it back and gain space with h4-h5.
5...Bg6 — The bishop retreats to g6 where it's still active on the diagonal.
6.h4 — The thematic advance! White threatens h5, trapping the bishop or forcing it to a passive square.
6...h6 — Preventing h5. Black must spend a tempo but the bishop stays active.
7.Nf3 — Developing naturally. White has a space advantage on the kingside.
7...Nd7 — Flexible development. The knight goes to d7 (not c6) to keep the c-file clear and prepare ...Ngf6 or ...e6.
3.e5 — The Advance Variation. White gains space but locks the center. Black will attack the pawn chain with ...c5 and ...f6.
3...Bf5 — Developing the bishop outside the pawn chain before playing ...e6. This is the standard and strongest approach.
4...e6 — Locking in the pawn structure. Black will now work on undermining d4 and e5.
5.Be2 — A modern, flexible approach. White develops quietly and prepares to castle.
5...Nd7 — Preparing ...Ne7 and ...c5. The knight goes to d7 to support the ...c5 break.
6.O-O — White castles. The position is solid for both sides.
6...Ne7 — Heading for f5 or g6. Black has a solid position and clear plans: ...c5, ...Nc8-b6, or ...Ng6 targeting e5.
3.exd5 — The Exchange Variation. Considered drawish at the top level but still requires accurate play from Black.
3...cxd5 — Recapturing. The position is symmetrical but White has a slight initiative.
4.Bd3 — Developing actively, eyeing the h7 square.
5.c3 — Reinforcing d4. White aims for a solid center.
6.Bf4 — Active development. The bishop controls the c1-h6 diagonal.
6...Bg4 — Pinning the knight (once it develops to f3). Black creates counterplay.
7.Nf3 — Completing development. The position is roughly equal but both sides have clear plans. White aims for a minority attack on the queenside, Black targets the isolated d4 pawn if it becomes one.
3.f3 — The Fantasy Variation! An aggressive, offbeat line. White wants to build a massive center with e4-d4-f3 and later Bc4 + Ne2.
3...dxe4 — The best response. Black takes the pawn and challenges White to prove compensation.
4.fxe4 — Recapturing. White has a big center but the king is exposed (no f-pawn shield).
4...e5! — The key counter-strike! Black immediately challenges the center before White can consolidate.
5...Bg4 — Pinning the knight and increasing pressure on the center.
6...Nd7 — Flexible development. Black keeps options open for ...Ngf6 or ...Nb6. The position is dynamically balanced — White has the center but Black has better development and targets.
4...Nf6 — The Two Knights line. Black invites the knight trade, accepting doubled f-pawns for the bishop pair and open g-file.
5.Nxf6+ — White takes the trade, doubling Black's pawns.
5...gxf6 — Recapturing toward the center. The doubled pawns look ugly but Black gets the bishop pair and an open g-file for attacking.
6...Bf5 — The bishop comes out before ...e6 locks it in. Classic Caro-Kann strategy.
7.Ne2 — Flexible. The knight can go to g3 to attack the bishop or to f4.
8...Bd6 — Black has a solid position with the bishop pair. Plans include ...Qc7, ...O-O-O, and using the open g-file. The doubled f-pawns control key central squares (e5, g5).
Starting position. This line shows how to handle the critical ...c5 break in the Advance.
5...c5 — The thematic break! Black strikes at the d4 base of White's pawn chain. This is the key strategic idea in the Advance Caro-Kann.
6...Nc6 — Developing and adding pressure to d4.
7.c3 — Reinforcing d4. White wants to maintain the pawn chain.
8.cxd4 — Recapturing. White has an IQP but active pieces.
8...Nge7 — Heading for f5 to blockade the IQP. Black has successfully broken through and has a comfortable position with clear targets.
4.c4 — The Panov Attack! White immediately challenges Black's d5 pawn, creating an asymmetrical position. This transforms the game into an IQP structure.
5...e6 — Solidifying d5. Black accepts a slightly passive but very solid structure.
6...Bb4 — Pinning the knight! This is the most active response, putting pressure on c3 and indirectly on d4.
7.Bd3 — Developing the bishop to its best square. White has an IQP position with active pieces. Black's plan is to trade pieces and exploit the isolated pawn in the endgame.
4...Nd7 — The flexible alternative to 4...Bf5. Black keeps options open — the knight can go to gf6 and the bishop can still come to f5 later.
5...Ngf6 — Now the knight develops. Black aims to trade the centralized Ne4.
7...Bf5 — The bishop finally comes out. Black has achieved a solid, harmonious position with no weaknesses. This is the appeal of the ...Nd7 system — flexibility.
Starting position. This line shows how the Caro-Kann naturally punishes Scholar's Mate attempts.
1...c6 — The Caro-Kann. Even against dubious White play, this setup is rock-solid.
2.Bc4?! — An inaccurate move, aiming for a Scholar's Mate setup. But the Caro-Kann structure makes this ineffective.
2...d5 — Attacking the bishop immediately! This is why Bc4 is premature.
3.Bb3 — The bishop retreats. White has wasted two tempi.
3...dxe4 — Black wins a pawn! The e4 pawn is undefended.
4.Qh5 — White tries the Scholar's Mate idea anyway, threatening Qxf7#.
4...g6! — Simply blocking the queen's attack on f7. There is no mate.
5.Qe2 — White tries to recover the pawn.
5...Nf6 — Defending e4 and developing. Black is a pawn up with a better position. The Scholar's Mate attempt has completely backfired.
4.Nc3 — The Short/Shirov variation. Instead of Nf3, White develops the knight to c3, preparing an aggressive g4 push.
5.g4 — Aggressive! White pushes the bishop back and gains kingside space. This is the point of 4.Nc3.
5...Bg6 — The bishop retreats. It's still useful on this diagonal.
6.Nge2 — Developing the knight to e2 (not f3) to support g4 and prepare Nf4.
6...c5 — The critical counter-break! Black must act before White consolidates the kingside.
7.h4 — Continuing the kingside advance. White threatens h5, trapping the bishop.
7...h5! — The key defensive resource! Black prevents h5 and creates counterplay. After ...hxg4, the h-file opens for Black's rook. A sharp, double-edged position.
